---
title: "Volatility Forecasting with GARCH Models"
subtitle: "Netflix (NFLX) Stock Returns"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
  echo: true
jupyter: python3
---

## Introduction

This report presents a reproducible pipeline for modelling and forecasting the
volatility of Netflix (NFLX) daily stock returns.  Three GARCH-family
specifications are compared — GARCH(1,1), GJR-GARCH and EGARCH — and the best
model (by AIC) is used for rolling out-of-sample variance forecasting.

```{python}
import sys
import warnings

sys.path.insert(0, "src")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from volatility_forecasting.data import DataLoader
from volatility_forecasting.models import VolatilityModel, compare_models
from volatility_forecasting.forecasting import RollingForecaster
from volatility_forecasting.plots import (
    plot_returns,
    plot_conditional_volatility,
    plot_forecast_vs_realized,
    plot_residuals_diagnostics,
    plot_model_comparison,
)
```

## Data

Daily prices are downloaded from Yahoo Finance.  Log returns are computed as
$r_t = \ln(P_t / P_{t-1})$.

```{python}
loader = DataLoader(ticker="NFLX")
df = loader.get_processed_data()

print(f"Period       : {df['date'].min().date()} to {df['date'].max().date()}")
print(f"Observations : {len(df)}")
df[["date", "close", "log_return"]].describe()
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Daily log returns exhibit pronounced volatility clustering."
plot_returns(df["date"], df["log_return"])
```

## Model Fitting

Returns are scaled to percentage points (×100) before estimation.

```{python}
returns = df["log_return"] * 100

garch = VolatilityModel(returns, "GARCH")
garch.fit()
print(garch.result.summary().tables[1])
```

```{python}
gjr = VolatilityModel(returns, "GJR-GARCH")
gjr.fit()
print(gjr.result.summary().tables[1])
```

```{python}
egarch = VolatilityModel(returns, "EGARCH")
egarch.fit()
print(egarch.result.summary().tables[1])
```

## Model Comparison

```{python}
comparison = compare_models(returns)
comparison
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Information-criteria comparison across the three specifications."
plot_model_comparison(comparison)
```

## Diagnostics

The Ljung-Box test checks whether standardised residuals still contain
autocorrelation.  A p-value above 0.05 indicates an adequate fit.

```{python}
best_name = comparison.index[0]
models_dict = {"GARCH": garch, "GJR-GARCH": gjr, "EGARCH": egarch}
best = models_dict[best_name]

print(f"Best model by AIC: {best_name}\n")
best.ljung_box_test()
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of standardised residuals versus the standard normal."
plot_residuals_diagnostics(best.get_standardized_residuals(), best_name)
```

## Conditional Volatility

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "Estimated conditional volatility over the full sample period."
plot_conditional_volatility(
    df["date"], best.result.conditional_volatility, best_name
)
```

## Rolling Forecast

A rolling window of 500 observations is re-estimated every 10 trading days
to produce one-step-ahead variance forecasts.

```{python}
forecaster = RollingForecaster(returns, model_type=best_name, window=500)
fc = forecaster.run(step=10)
print(f"Forecast points: {len(fc)}")
```

```{python}
#| fig-cap: "One-step-ahead variance forecast versus realised squared returns."
fc_dates = df["date"].iloc[fc["t"].values].values
plot_forecast_vs_realized(fc, dates=fc_dates, model_name=best_name)
```

```{python}
print(f"MSE : {RollingForecaster.mse(fc):.4f}")
print(f"MAE : {RollingForecaster.mae(fc):.4f}")
```

## Conclusions

The analysis confirms that NFLX daily returns display strong volatility
clustering.  Among the three GARCH-family models tested, the specification
with the lowest AIC provides the best in-sample fit, and the Ljung-Box test
shows no significant remaining autocorrelation in standardised residuals.
The rolling out-of-sample forecast captures the time-varying nature of
volatility, though point accuracy is inherently limited for daily equity
returns.
